Adjusted EBITDA and Margins Continue to Improve

Profitability on an adjusted basis moved in the right direction, with adjusted EBITDA up 7.2% year over year to $75.2 million. The adjusted EBITDA margin expanded to 28.7%, up from 27.5% in the prior-year quarter, a 1.2 percentage-point improvement. Management credited disciplined cost control and operating leverage, even as the company invested in technology, AI initiatives, and support infrastructure. The expanding margin profile was presented as evidence that Phoenix can grow while still improving unit economics.

Adjusted Net Income and EPS Edge Higher

Adjusted net income attributable to the company rose 5.3% to $53.6 million, from $50.9 million in the prior-year period, while adjusted diluted EPS ticked up by $0.03 to $1.38. Executives argued that these adjusted metrics better reflect the underlying performance of the education business, especially given the quarter’s large noncash equity compensation charges and cyber-related costs. The growth in adjusted earnings, even as the company invests in strategic initiatives, was framed as a key marker of sustainable progress.

Balance Sheet Strength and Conservative Capital Structure

Phoenix Education Partners closed the quarter with $218.1 million in cash, restricted cash, and marketable securities, up from $194.8 million at the end of August 2025—a $23.3 million increase. Operating cash flow was a solid $31.1 million, and capex was modest at $4.7 million. With no outstanding debt or borrowings on its revolving credit facility, the company sits on a conservative balance sheet that management says provides ample flexibility for investment, risk management, and shareholder returns.

Dividend Launch Signals Confidence in Cash Generation

In a notable move for investors, the board declared the company’s first regular quarterly cash dividend, set at approximately $0.21 per share, or roughly $0.84 on an annualized basis. Management presented the dividend as a tangible signal of confidence in Phoenix’s recurring cash generation and as a complement to its disciplined capital allocation strategy. The decision suggests a shift toward returning capital to shareholders alongside continued internal investment and opportunistic growth spending.

Regulatory Data Trend Favorably, but Watchpoints Remain

On the regulatory front, Phoenix reported a broadly constructive update from the U.S. Department of Education’s negotiated rulemaking process. Preliminary accountability metrics showed that all University of Phoenix programs with available data are passing, with earnings data covering more than half of its programs. Management argued this validates the institution’s focus on career-aligned offerings and outcomes. However, they cautioned that the rulemaking is not yet finalized and noted that certain fields, such as behavioral sciences, could face more scrutiny under earnings-based measures.

AI Integration and Operational Efficiencies Take Shape

The company spotlighted progress on several AI initiatives, both in the classroom and behind the scenes. The university is embedding AI into its curriculum to improve student fluency with emerging tools, positioning its programs closer to evolving workplace demands. Operationally, Phoenix is piloting large language model–based chat assistance, AI-enabled appointment setting, and automated outreach to prospective and current students. Management expects these initiatives to enhance enrollment conversion, improve retention, and deliver more responsive 24/7 support, ultimately bolstering both student outcomes and margins.

B2B Expansion and Broad-Based Program Demand

Employer-affiliated enrollments not only grew but also made a meaningful contribution to overall enrollment gains, affirming Phoenix’s strategy to deepen B2B relationships. Management highlighted that more than 90% of its programs are aligned with current market needs, which is central to maintaining employer interest and student relevance. Active account management and efforts to expand within existing employer partners are expected to drive further penetration, reinforcing the company’s shift toward more stable, employer-tied demand versus purely consumer-driven channels.

GAAP Net Income Hit by One-Time and Noncash Items

In contrast to the stronger adjusted figures, GAAP net income attributable to the company fell sharply to $15.5 million, or $0.40 diluted EPS, from $46.4 million, or $1.23 diluted EPS, a year earlier. Management attributed this decline primarily to sizable noncash share-based compensation charges and IPO-related items in the quarter. The discrepancy between GAAP and adjusted results underscores earnings volatility around the IPO and compensation restructuring, a dynamic investors will need to parse when evaluating the underlying health of the business.

Nonrecurring Stock-Based Compensation Depresses GAAP Earnings

A key driver of the GAAP earnings drop was $29.5 million of noncash share-based compensation recorded in the quarter, of which roughly $23 million stemmed from modifications to pre-IPO stock options. These expenses were excluded from adjusted EBITDA and other non-GAAP measures. Management reiterated that these charges are expected to be largely nonrecurring, framing them as a technical artifact of going public rather than an ongoing drag on profitability.

Cybersecurity Incident and Associated Costs

The company also booked $4.5 million of expenses tied to a cyber incident originating from a zero-day vulnerability in Oracle E-Business Suite. Costs included notification efforts, third-party cybersecurity support, and legal services. While some additional related expenses are anticipated in coming periods, management emphasized that Phoenix maintains comprehensive cyber insurance coverage and has strengthened its defenses. The episode underscores the operational and reputational risks facing digital-heavy education providers, even as management framed the financial impact as manageable.

Higher Instructional and G&A Costs Driven by Equity Charges

Instructional expenses climbed by $7.1 million to $115.2 million, while general and administrative spending jumped $24.6 million to $106.6 million year over year. Executives noted that much of this increase traces back to the elevated stock-based compensation recognized in the quarter, rather than structural cost inflation. Adjusted metrics, which strip out these noncash items, showed improved margins, supporting management’s argument that underlying cost discipline remains intact.

Revenue Lags Enrollment Amid Mix and Timing Effects

Despite healthy enrollment growth, revenue expansion was more muted, reflecting both mix and timing dynamics. The growing share of employer-affiliated students—who benefit from higher tuition discounts—naturally pressures revenue per student. In addition, lingering effects from last year’s surge in short-duration “risk-free” enrollments altered the revenue recognition pattern. Management cautioned that this enrollment-to-revenue lag may persist into the second and third quarters but expects trends to normalize by the fourth quarter, with revenue growth better reflecting the underlying enrollment base.

Fraud Mitigation Efforts Ongoing Despite Progress

Management reiterated that fraud in the broader higher-education market remains an ongoing risk, but emphasized that Phoenix has made tangible progress in detection and deterrence. By pushing verification processes to the top of the enrollment funnel, the company reports not only reduced fraud levels but also improved productivity among enrollment representatives, who can focus more on legitimate prospects. Even so, leadership stressed that vigilance must continue, as bad actors adapt and compliance expectations evolve.

Regulatory Uncertainty Persists Until Rules Are Finalized

While early data and negotiated rulemaking outcomes appear favorable, management acknowledged that regulatory risk has not fully cleared. Final rules from the Department of Education could still influence program economics, particularly in disciplines that historically generate lower earnings outcomes for graduates. The company does not currently anticipate material adverse effects, but it is monitoring developments closely and emphasized its ability to adjust program mix and support structures if needed.

Guidance Reaffirmed on the Back of a “Solid” Start

Phoenix Education Partners reaffirmed its fiscal 2026 outlook, projecting net revenue between $1.025 billion and $1.035 billion and adjusted EBITDA in the range of $244 million to $249 million. The company framed the first quarter—marked by 2.9% revenue growth, 4.1% enrollment growth, and a 28.7% adjusted EBITDA margin—as a “solid” start that aligns well with its full-year plan. Management expects the near-term drag from mix and timing factors to ease over the course of the year, while the balance sheet strength, cash generation, and the new dividend policy signal confidence that these targets are achievable despite lingering regulatory and cyber-related uncertainties.

Phoenix Education Partners’ latest earnings call painted a picture of a business on firmer operational footing, even as headline GAAP earnings are muddied by one-off and noncash items. Enrollment and adjusted profitability are trending upward, employer partnerships are scaling, and regulatory and fraud-related challenges appear manageable so far. For investors, the combination of steady growth, improving margins, a strong balance sheet, and the initiation of a regular dividend positions the stock as a potentially attractive way to play the ongoing evolution of career-focused, digitally delivered higher education—provided the company continues to execute on risk management and regulatory navigation in the quarters ahead.
